primary_bug.summary: The new kube-proxy NodeManager makes NodeIP acquisition a HARD, FATAL prerequisite at startup, removing the previous non-fatal 'fall back to localhost and keep running' behavior — a backward-compatibility regression. In newNodeManager (pkg/proxy/node.go), when the node object never gets NodeIPs, the 5-minute PollUntilContextCancel times out and the function does `if pollErr != nil { return nil, err }`; newProxyServer (cmd/kube-proxy/app/server.go) then does `s.NodeManager, err = proxy.NewNodeManager(...); if err != nil { return nil, err }`, so kube-proxy ABORTS startup. The replaced code (getNodeIPs: a ~30s bounded exponential backoff that returned possibly-empty IPs, then detectNodeIPs falling back to localhost) was NON-fatal and let kube-proxy start. In environments where the Node exists but NodeIPs are assigned asynchronously/late by an external cloud provider (cloud-provider-azure, kubernetes-sigs/cloud-provider-azure#9266), kube-proxy now hangs up to 5 minutes and then hard-fails to start instead of starting with the localhost fallback.
primary_bug.locus: newNodeManager: the poll-then-`if pollErr != nil { return nil, err }` NodeIP path (~L108), propagated by cmd/kube-proxy/app/server.go newProxyServer `if err != nil { return nil, err }` (~L215-219). It also uses a 5-minute timeout for NodeIPs where the old code used ~30s.
primary_bug.must_flag: A reviewer catches the primary bug if it flags that NodeIP-fetch failure/timeout now ABORTS kube-proxy startup (NewNodeManager returns an error that newProxyServer propagates) where the previous behavior was NON-fatal — i.e., it removes/breaks the prior 'continue with a localhost fallback when NodeIPs are unavailable' behavior, a backward-compatibility regression for nodes whose IPs are populated late (external cloud providers). Equivalent framings that count: 'kube-proxy will now fail to start / block ~5 min if the node has no IPs yet, unlike before'; 'making NodeIP acquisition a hard blocking prerequisite breaks cloud-provider startup ordering'; 'the error return on timeout should be non-fatal / should fall back like the old getNodeIPs did'. Merely restating that a NodeManager was added, or praising the new timeout, does NOT count. NOTE the direction: the BUG is that it became fatal/blocking; the fix (#133059) makes it non-fatal again.
primary_bug.source: Reverted by #132958 ('Revert Kube proxy node manager'); re-landed as #133059 ('kube-proxy node manager (take 2)') 'with more care to exactly preserve backward-compatibility so as to fix cloud-provider-azure#9266'. The take-2 node.go carries the decisive comment: 'for backward compatibility, NewNodeManager doesn't return any error if it failed to retrieve NodeIPs and watchPodCIDRs is false ... we keep going even if we didn't find a node ... or it didn't have IPs', and restores a 30s NodeIP timeout. Foreshadowed pre-merge by reviewer danwinship: 'this loses the timeout that the previous version had (about 30 seconds)'.
primary_bug.difficulty: hard/subtle — requires understanding kube-proxy/cloud-provider startup ordering (Node exists before the CCM assigns IPs) and that the prior localhost fallback was load-bearing for backward compatibility. A maintainer (danwinship) foreshadowed it but it was marked resolved and shipped anyway; only reverted after a downstream (azure) breakage. Expect low recall; a genuine catch is a strong signal.

human_issues.summary: The NodeManager exits the process via exitFunc = os.Exit(1) directly (OnNodeChange on NodeIP/PodCIDR change, OnNodeDelete) instead of the previous klog.FlushAndExit(...), which can drop buffered logs on exit — making failures undiagnosable. Reported post-merge as breaking cluster creation.
human_issues.loc: pkg/proxy/node.go — NodeManager.exitFunc/os.Exit in OnNodeChange (~L155) and OnNodeDelete
human_issues.thread: nojnhuh (unresolved): 'this PR seems to have broken creating clusters for me. Is it possible the previous klog.FlushAndExit() may have been more robust in terms of making sure all logs get written on exit?'

human_issues.summary: server.go logs `Successfully retrieved NodeIPs` UNCONDITIONALLY (even when rawNodeIPs is empty), and there is no warning when NodeIPs come back empty — misleading/no diagnostic for the degraded case. (The take-2 fix guards the success log with `if len(rawNodeIPs) > 0` and logs an explicit timeout error otherwise.)
human_issues.loc: cmd/kube-proxy/app/server.go — newProxyServer, the `logger.Info("Successfully retrieved NodeIPs", ...)` line (~L219)
human_issues.thread: danwinship (unresolved): 'rawNodeIPs can be nil/empty here if it timed out, right? We need to log an error somewhere ... even if it's not fatal, it's unexpected and possibly bad.'

known_safe.summary: NodeManager.OnNodeChange calling exitFunc(1) when NodeIPs (or, in watchPodCIDRs mode, PodCIDRs) CHANGE after startup is INTENTIONAL by design — the type doc says it 'crashes kube-proxy if there are any changes in NodeIPs or PodCIDRs' so it restarts with fresh config (this mirrors the old NodePodCIDRHandler's klog.FlushAndExit-on-PodCIDR-change). Flagging 'kube-proxy crashes on node IP/PodCIDR change' as a defect is a false positive; the log-flush concern (h1) is the only real issue on that path.

NodeManager.Node() returning n.node.DeepCopy() under the mutex is intentional (callers must not mutate shared informer objects). Flagging the deep copy as a performance problem is a low-value nitpick, not a defect.

The `if s.NodeManager != nil` guard in Run() and the hollow-proxy not creating a NodeManager are intentional special-casing (hollow-proxy has no real node). Flagging a nil-NodeManager crash for the normal path is a false positive.

clusters.summary: newNodeManager returns an error on NodeIP-fetch timeout (up to 5 min) and newProxyServer propagates it, ABORTING kube-proxy startup when the node has no NodeIPs — removing the previous non-fatal ~30-63s backoff + localhost/BindAddress fallback that let kube-proxy start degraded. Backward-compat regression for cloud-provider environments (e.g. azure) where NodeIPs are populated late.
clusters.rationale: Nails it: newNodeManager returns err on NodeIP-fetch timeout and newProxyServer propagates it, aborting startup vs prior non-fatal backoff+localhost fallback; names the cloud-provider (azure) late-IP regression.

clusters.summary: kube-proxy now os.Exit(1)s on ANY NodeIP change and on node deletion in ALL DetectLocalMode configs (previously IP changes were ignored and node-delete only marked ineligible/503); the crash contract is wired externally in server.go rather than self-enforced, with no debounce.
clusters.rationale: Flags the exit-on-NodeIP-change / exit-on-delete which the type doc states is intentional (crash to restart with fresh config); factually accurate but targets by-design behavior — known_safe safe-exit-on-change.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Restating the PR's intended crash-on-change contract plus debounce/wiring opinions is design commentary with no one-shot in-repo fix.

clusters.summary: The exit-on-NodeIP-change uses order-sensitive reflect.DeepEqual over derived IPs with no debounce/normalization, so a benign address reorder or the normal bringup sequence (kubelet sets IPv4, CCM adds IPv6 later) triggers a spurious os.Exit(1) -> CrashLoopBackOff during cluster bringup / fleet-wide flaps.
clusters.rationale: Order-sensitive reflect.DeepEqual over GetNodeHostIPs output with no debounce; normal dual-stack bringup (kubelet v4 then CCM adds v6) flips DeepEqual and calls exitFunc(1) -> crashloop — a real bug distinct from the by-design exit-on-change.

clusters.summary: OnNodeChange assigns n.node = node BEFORE validating the new node's IPs; a transient address-less update stores the IP-less node and early-returns (only logging), poisoning the baseline so a later update restoring the original IPs is seen as a change and triggers a spurious exit — and the error early-return skips the NodeIP-change crash check (missed crash).
clusters.rationale: OnNodeChange sets n.node=node before GetNodeHostIPs validation; a transient IP-less update poisons the baseline (logs+returns) so a later IP-restore reads as a change -> spurious exit; error path also skips the change check.

clusters.summary: All three NodeManager exit sites use klog.Flush()+exitFunc(os.Exit) instead of the prior klog.FlushAndExit(ExitFlushTimeout,1); plain Flush() is unbounded/synchronous, so a stalled log sink can drop the exit-reason log or delay os.Exit. Reported post-merge as breaking cluster creation with no crash-reason logged.
clusters.rationale: All exit sites now use klog.Flush()+os.Exit vs prior klog.FlushAndExit(ExitFlushTimeout,1); can drop the exit-reason log; ties to nojnhuh's post-merge cluster-creation breakage (h1).

clusters.summary: The new self-induced os.Exit(1) paths (NodeIP change, PodCIDR change, node delete) emit no metric and no Kubernetes Event, only a klog line, so a fleet-wide correlated crashloop is operationally invisible.
clusters.rationale: True the os.Exit paths emit only a klog line (no metric/Event), but the replaced NodePodCIDRHandler also only logged+FlushAndExit — an enhancement request, not a regression/defect.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'Emit a metric/Event on self-exit' is an operational-observability opinion with no in-repo anchor; unbounded suggestion class.

clusters.summary: NewNodeConfig registers only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc (no AddFunc), so the informer's synthetic Add-replay to the late-registered handler is silently dropped; a NodeIP/PodCIDR change in the startup-window gap (between NewNodeManager's poll and handler registration in Run()) bypasses NodeManager's crash-on-drift safety net. Also contradicts the OnNodeChange doc promising creation events.
clusters.rationale: NewNodeConfig registers only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc (AddFunc dropped), so the synthetic Add-replay to the late-registered handler is swallowed and a drift in the poll->register gap can miss the crash-on-change net; also contradicts OnNodeChange's 'creation or modification' doc.

clusters.summary: NodeConfig/NodeTopologyConfig register handlers on the shared node informer that NewNodeManager ALREADY started+synced, racing the caller's RegisterEventHandler append to the unsynchronized eventHandlers slice (go test -race detectable); the async synthetic-Add replay can also be processed with zero handlers registered, permanently losing initial topology.
clusters.rationale: Run() registers NodeConfig/NodeTopologyConfig handlers on the informer NewNodeManager already Started+synced; the async synthetic-Add can run handleNodeEvent with zero handlers (dedup then swallows initial zone) and races the unlocked eventHandlers append — old code deliberately Started after registration.

clusters.summary: handleChangeNode (wired only to UpdateFunc) contains an unreachable cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch copied from the delete handler; tombstones are only delivered on the delete path, so it is dead/misleading code.
clusters.rationale: True: handleChangeNode (UpdateFunc-only) carries a DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch that only ever fires on the delete path — dead/misleading code copied from the delete handler, but harmless.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: handleChangeNode is wired only to UpdateFunc, where client-go's documented contract never delivers DeletedFinalStateUnknown; the tombstone branch added by this PR is unreachable dead code, one-shot removal.

clusters.summary: NodeTopologyConfig's DeleteFunc is a no-op (topology labels never cleared on node delete) while handleNodeEvent's doc claims it handles Delete; the stale zone routing is masked only because NodeManager.OnNodeDelete crashes the process first.
clusters.rationale: True: NodeTopologyConfig DeleteFunc is a no-op while handleNodeEvent's doc says it handles Delete; but stale zone labels never materialize because OnNodeDelete exitFunc(1)s the process first — doc/coverage nit, masked.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: handleNodeEvent's new doc says it handles 'Add, Update and Delete' but DeleteFunc is a no-op that never calls it; objective doc/code mismatch on new code, one-shot doc fix.

clusters.summary: NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned but never read — the type has no Run()/sync-wait method (unlike sibling NodeConfig), so it is dead state implying a sync barrier that doesn't exist.
clusters.rationale: True: NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned handlerRegistration.HasSynced but never read (no Run()/sync-wait method) — dead state, trivial.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: listerSynced is assigned from HasSynced and never read (NodeTopologyConfig has no Run/sync-wait); dead state introduced by the PR implying a barrier that doesn't exist, one-shot removal.

clusters.summary: NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent publishes its internal topologyLabels map by reference to every proxier (and topologyLabels is read/written without a lock), with no defensive copy or ownership contract — safe only by an unwritten never-mutate-after-publish convention.
clusters.rationale: handleNodeEvent publishes n.topologyLabels by reference, but it always allocates a fresh map and never mutates a published one, and runs on the single informer goroutine — safe by convention, no live race; robustness nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: handleNodeEvent allocates a fresh map per event and never mutates after publish; the missing-ownership-contract concern is speculative with no concrete race pathway.

clusters.summary: The set of proxy-relevant topology labels is split across two packages — producer filter (handleNodeEvent, hardcoded LabelTopologyZone) and consumer (CategorizeEndpoints) — coupled only by a one-directional prose comment, risking silent mis-routing if they drift.
clusters.rationale: Producer filter (handleNodeEvent hardcodes LabelTopologyZone) and consumer (CategorizeEndpoints) are coupled only by the prose comment this PR added; a real drift risk but a design/coherence nit, not a defect.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Cross-package coupling risk is an architecture opinion with no one-shot fix; the diff itself added the prose cross-reference as its mitigation.

clusters.summary: config.NodeConfig's informer->handler dispatch (handleChangeNode/handleDeleteNode/Run) has ZERO unit-test coverage before or after this PR, despite the PR changing it non-trivially (merged Add/Update, dropped AddFunc).
clusters.rationale: True: NodeConfig's handleChangeNode/handleDeleteNode/Run have no direct unit test despite the PR merging Add/Update and dropping AddFunc — coverage gap, low value.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'Zero coverage for NodeConfig dispatch' is an unbounded add-tests-for-this-component ask, not a specific one-shot case for load-bearing new behavior.

clusters.summary: NodeConfig's struct doc comment describes a channel-based set/add/remove API that no longer exists (informer-driven now); pre-existing but sits above the rewritten code.
clusters.rationale: NodeConfig's 'accepts set/add/remove via channels' struct doc is stale (informer-driven), but it's pre-existing and unchanged by this diff — out-of-scope nit (self-acknowledged).
clusters.regrade_rationale: NodeConfig's channels-based doc was already false before this PR and the doc line itself is untouched context; pre-existing doc rot, not diff-caused.

clusters.summary: PodCIDRs() (and OnNodeChange storing the informer-owned *v1.Node without a DeepCopy) return/hold live references into the shared informer-cache object, while Node() DeepCopy()s and NodeIPs() allocates fresh — asymmetric encapsulation / latent aliasing of client-go's read-only cache.
clusters.rationale: PodCIDRs() returns n.node.Spec.PodCIDRs and OnNodeChange stores the informer-owned *Node without DeepCopy while Node() DeepCopies — a real asymmetry, but informer objects are effectively read-only/replaced so practical aliasing risk is low.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Speculative aliasing of client-go cache objects with no concrete mutation pathway; nothing mutates the shared node, so this is design taste.

clusters.summary: newNodeManager discards the poll's own pollErr and returns a side-channel domain err, relying on the implicit invariant that err is non-nil on timeout (true today via immediate=true); a future poll-branch could return (nil, nil), nil-panicking the caller. On context cancellation it also surfaces a misleading domain error instead of the cancellation.
clusters.rationale: The (nil,nil) path is currently safe (immediate=true guarantees err set before any false,nil); framed as future fragility/implicit-invariant + misleading domain err on cancellation — a nitpick per the answer key, not a live panic.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Self-admits err is always non-nil today (immediate=true); 'a future poll-branch could return nil,nil' is speculative robustness with no concrete pathway.

clusters.summary: newNodeManager calls thisNodeInformerFactory.Start(wait.NeverStop) before its error returns and never stores/stops it, so on either error-return path the reflector/watch goroutines leak (bounded only by process exit; also accumulates across the ~13 test constructions).
clusters.rationale: thisNodeInformerFactory.Start(NeverStop) is never stopped, so on the error returns the reflector goroutines leak and accumulate across test constructions — but on success the informer is meant to run for process life, and on prod error the process exits; low value.
clusters.regrade_rationale: The error-path informer goroutine leak is inert in production (fatal error exits the process) and Start(wait.NeverStop) is the surrounding idiom (server.go does the same); a lifecycle fix would invite debate.

clusters.summary: The '5 minutes for PodCIDR allocation' comment now understates scope: the same timeout also fatally bounds waiting for node existence and NodeIP readiness in ALL modes (previously a ~30-60s non-fatal wait).
clusters.rationale: Framed as a comment-scope issue but explicitly states the 5-min timeout now 'fatally bounds ... NodeIP readiness in ALL modes (previously a ~30-60s non-fatal wait)' — the fatal-vs-nonfatal regression per must_flag.

clusters.summary: The NodeManager type doc comment is a grammatically garbled run-on (duplicated 'handles' verb, merge artifact) and omits that node deletion also unconditionally crashes kube-proxy.
clusters.rationale: True: the NodeManager type doc is a garbled run-on ('...NodeIPs and PodCIDRs handles node watch events...', duplicated 'handles') and omits that delete crashes — grammar/doc nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: 'based on the NodeIPs and PodCIDRs handles node watch events' is an objectively garbled run-on with a duplicated verb (calibration: broken grammar qualifies).

clusters.summary: NodeManager takes a ctx in newNodeManager but logs via package-global klog.InfoS/ErrorS instead of a ctx-scoped logger, unlike every sibling config.*Config type added in this PR, dropping contextual keys.
clusters.rationale: True: NodeManager logs via package-global klog.InfoS/ErrorS rather than a ctx-scoped logger like sibling *Config types (which store klog.FromContext(ctx)); dropped contextual keys — style/consistency nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: NodeManager logs via package-global klog.InfoS/ErrorS while NodeTopologyConfig (added in this PR) and NodeConfig use ctx-derived loggers; the PR's own sibling convention (calibration example).

clusters.summary: The issues.k8s.io/111321 reference explaining why a PodCIDR change forces a full process restart was deleted with no replacement, and the new crash triggers (NodeIP change, node deletion) carry no rationale.
clusters.rationale: True: the //issues.k8s.io/111321 rationale for restart-on-PodCIDR-change was deleted and the new NodeIP/delete crash triggers carry no rationale — knowledge-preservation nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: both issues.k8s.io/111321 references are deleted and no rationale replaces them; re-adding the link to NodeManager's doc is a finite one-shot knowledge-preservation fix anchored in the file's own deleted text.

clusters.summary: OnNodeChange has no return after the PodCIDR-change exitFunc(1), so execution falls through to the NodeIP check and can call exitFunc(1) twice in one event — inert in production (os.Exit never returns) but wrong with an injected/test exitFunc.
clusters.rationale: True: no return after the watchPodCIDRs exitFunc(1), so it falls through to the NodeIP check — but inert in production (os.Exit never returns); only matters with an injected test exitFunc.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified fall-through after exitFunc(1) in the PodCIDR branch; with the PR's own injected-exitFunc contract execution continues past 'exit' (possible double-exit/spurious error log); single 'return' fix maintainers would take.

clusters.summary: NodeIPs() and OnNodeChange's oldNodeIPs both discard the GetNodeHostIPs error via `_`, unlike the file's other call sites that check it, conflating 'no IPs' with 'lookup failed'.
clusters.rationale: True: NodeIPs() and OnNodeChange's oldNodeIPs discard the GetNodeHostIPs error via _, unlike the poll loop; conflates no-IPs with lookup-failed — low-value code-quality inconsistency.
clusters.regrade_rationale: The '_' discards are internally consistent (error treated as no-IPs) and any fix (log? return error? change API) invites maintainer debate rather than a debate-free one-shot patch.

clusters.summary: OnNodeSynced's doc comment claims 'all pre-existing Nodes have been reported' but the informer is field-selected to a single node and OnNodeChange never receives an Add (no AddFunc); it also breaks the sibling 'X is a handler for Node Y' phrasing.
clusters.rationale: True: OnNodeSynced doc says 'all pre-existing Nodes have been reported' though the informer is single-node field-selected and there's no AddFunc; minor doc inaccuracy/style break.
clusters.regrade_rationale: New OnNodeSynced doc claims 'all pre-existing Nodes have been reported' but the informer is single-node field-selected and NodeConfig dropped AddFunc so nothing is reported; objectively inaccurate new doc, one-shot reword.

clusters.summary: NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() doc comments say values were 'polled in NewNodeManager()' implying a one-time snapshot, but OnNodeChange overwrites n.node so both actually return the current, live-mutated node's values.
clusters.rationale: True: NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() docs say 'polled in NewNodeManager()' implying a snapshot, but OnNodeChange overwrites n.node so they return live values — misleading doc, no functional impact.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified: NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() docs say 'polled in NewNodeManager()' while OnNodeChange overwrites n.node; objective doc/code mismatch on new code (calibration example).

clusters.summary: No concurrency/-race test exercises NodeManager's shared n.node field (written by the informer goroutine, read by the healthz handler) or OnNodeChange concurrently with the accessors.
clusters.rationale: True: no -race test exercises concurrent n.node access; but all accessors take n.mu, so it's a coverage gap not a bug — low value.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'No -race/concurrency test' is the calibration's explicit trivia example; unbounded test-ask class.

clusters.summary: newNodeManager's 'can not sync node informer' (WaitForNamedCacheSync failure) branch is not exercised by any test.
clusters.rationale: True: the WaitForNamedCacheSync-failure ('can not sync node informer') branch has no test — minor coverage gap.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'Cache-sync failure branch untested' is the calibration's explicit trivia example; not load-bearing behavior.

clusters.summary: OnNodeChange's transient GetNodeHostIPs-error early-return path ('node loses all NodeIPs') is untested — every TestNodeManagerOnNodeChange case supplies a valid-IP updated node.
clusters.rationale: True: every TestNodeManagerOnNodeChange case supplies valid updated IPs, so the GetNodeHostIPs-error early-return (where the c4 bug lives) is untested — coverage gap, but framed only as 'untested'.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Untested transient error-path (log+return) is the untested-branch class, not load-bearing behavior; unbounded add-a-case ask.

clusters.summary: NodeEligible() (invoked on every /healthz + liveness probe) acquires an exclusive hs.lock.Lock() (upgraded from RLock) that no longer guards any field it accesses — needlessly serializing against the proxier sync loop — and deep-copies the entire v1.Node via Node() to read only DeletionTimestamp/Taints.
clusters.rationale: NodeEligible() upgraded RLock->Lock (guards no field it uses now) and calls Node() which DeepCopies the whole v1.Node on every /healthz+liveness probe to read DeletionTimestamp/Taints — a real perf regression this PR introduced.

clusters.summary: NodeEligible()/Node() unconditionally dereference hs.nodeManager with no nil guard, replacing the old nil-safe default-eligible bool; a nil-deref landmine (non-nil only by construction today).
clusters.rationale: hs.nodeManager is dereferenced with no nil guard, but it is non-nil by construction on the normal path (self-described as a 'landmine ... non-nil only by construction today') — fragility nitpick, not a refutable bug.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Nil-deref 'landmine' concedes nodeManager is non-nil by construction on every real path; speculative robustness with no concrete pathway.

clusters.summary: ProxyHealthServer now depends on the concrete, process-killing *proxy.NodeManager solely to call Node(), inverting the previous proxy->healthcheck dependency direction and adding a healthcheck->proxy import edge, instead of a narrow one-method interface.
clusters.rationale: True that healthcheck now imports pkg/proxy and holds concrete *proxy.NodeManager (dependency direction inverted vs old node.go->healthcheck) instead of a one-method interface — a coupling/design observation, not a functional defect.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Concrete-type-vs-interface and dependency-direction concerns are architecture opinions that invite debate; no debate-free one-shot patch.

clusters.summary: ProxyHealthServer's type doc item 3 still describes the removed SyncNode push model, but this PR deletes SyncNode in favor of on-demand NodeEligible() pulling from nodeManager.Node().
clusters.rationale: Plausible: SyncNode was removed in favor of pull-based NodeEligible(), so a ProxyHealthServer type-doc item describing the SyncNode push model would be stale; the doc text isn't in the shown hunks so lower confidence.
clusters.regrade_rationale: PR deletes SyncNode from proxy_health.go but leaves the type doc's 'sync node status' item untouched; diff-caused doc/code mismatch, one-shot doc fix.

clusters.summary: s.podCIDRs is now populated for ALL DetectLocalMode values (previously gated to LocalModeNodeCIDR), reactivating checkBadConfig/checkBadIPConfig podCIDR IP-family warnings for modes where the field was previously nil; the '// only used for LocalModeNodeCIDR' comment is now stale.
clusters.rationale: s.podCIDRs = NodeManager.PodCIDRs() is now unconditional (was gated to LocalModeNodeCIDR in platformSetup); the '// only used for LocalModeNodeCIDR' field comment is now stale and the field is populated in all modes — a real diff-verifiable behavior change.

clusters.summary: server.go logs 'Successfully retrieved NodeIPs' unconditionally even when rawNodeIPs is empty, with no warning for the degraded/empty case — danwinship's request for coherent warn-on-empty logging was only partially addressed.
clusters.rationale: server.go logs 'Successfully retrieved NodeIPs' unconditionally with no warn-on-empty — exactly danwinship's empty-NodeIPs logging concern (h2).

clusters.summary: The comment claiming handlers must be registered before starting the informer 'or we'll lose events' is factually incorrect (informers replay to late-registered handlers); danwinship flagged it and it remains.
clusters.rationale: The only such comment ('start after ... configure the shared informer event handler first') was DELETED by this PR, not retained; and late-registration replay is exactly what creates the real c8 race, so calling it 'factually incorrect' misreads it.

clusters.summary: winkernel's TODO comment says 'implement OnTopologyChanged' but the actual interface method is OnTopologyChange (no trailing 'd'); placement also makes godoc render the TODO as the method doc.
clusters.rationale: True: winkernel TODO says 'OnTopologyChanged' while the method is OnTopologyChange, and the TODO sits above the doc line so godoc renders it — trivial comment nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified in winkernel/proxier.go: TODO says 'OnTopologyChanged' but the method is OnTopologyChange; objective identifier mismatch, one-shot (calibration example).

clusters.summary: metaproxier/winkernel OnTopologyChange doc comments use different (third) wording than the verbatim-identical comments on iptables/ipvs/nftables; one also says it 'provides the observed change' implying a delta when it's the full current label set.
clusters.rationale: True: metaproxier/winkernel OnTopologyChange doc wording differs from the identical iptables/ipvs/nftables comments, and the interface doc's 'provides the observed change' implies a delta though it's the full label set — doc-consistency nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Comment-wording harmonization across proxiers is taste; unlike the klog sibling case there is no functional divergence, and the fix is churn that invites debate.

clusters.summary: The new cross-reference comment in topology.go has a double-space typo and imprecise wording ('watch other endpoint types or labels').
clusters.rationale: True: the added topology.go comment has a double space ('to  watch other endpoint types or labels') — pure typo.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Confirmed 'to  watch' double-space in the new topology.go comment; objective typo in new code, converges (calibration example).

clusters.summary: OnTopologyChange is untested across the proxiers; metaProxier's ipv4/ipv6 fan-out — the one implementation with real logic — has no dedicated test.
clusters.rationale: True: OnTopologyChange has no per-proxier test and metaProxier's v4/v6 fan-out is untested — but the logic is a trivial delegation; low value.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'OnTopologyChange untested across proxiers' is coverage-ask class; metaProxier's fan-out is trivial two-line delegation, not load-bearing logic.

clusters.summary: TestNewNodeTopologyConfig's mock records only the last topologyLabels value with no invocation counter, so its 'no notification' negative assertions are vacuous — deleting the reflect.DeepEqual dedup guard it exists to test would not fail any assertion; a final case also asserts only require.Len(...,1).
clusters.rationale: True: the topology mock records only the last value with no call counter and the callback fires per-event regardless of notification, so the require.Empty negatives are weak — test-quality nit.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Verified by tracing every case: deleting the reflect.DeepEqual dedup guard fails no assertion (mock records only the last value), so the new test's negative assertions are vacuous for the exact feature it exists to test; one-shot fix is an invocation counter.

clusters.summary: healthcheck_test.go constructs NodeManager via the exported NewNodeManager (hard-wired to real os.Exit) and discards its error, so a future makeNode change touching addresses could kill the test binary, and a construction failure becomes a confusing nil-deref instead of a clear failure.
clusters.rationale: True: healthcheck_test builds NodeManager via NewNodeManager (real os.Exit) and discards err, so a future makeNode address change could kill the binary and a nil manager would nil-deref — real test fragility, but no current failure (makeNode is deterministic).
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'A future makeNode change could kill the test binary' is speculative test robustness; today's construction succeeds and the discarded error is inert.

clusters.summary: TestNewNodeManager synchronizes its update-injecting goroutine with the poll loop via time.Sleep handoffs racing the 10ms poll interval / 1s timeout — a wall-clock coordination anti-pattern that is a latent CI-flake risk.
clusters.rationale: True: TestNewNodeManager coordinates its injector goroutine with time.Sleep handoffs racing the 10ms poll/1s timeout — a wall-clock anti-pattern / latent CI flake, low severity.
clusters.regrade_rationale: Wall-clock sleep coordination is a latent-flake speculation; restructuring the goroutine handoff is not a specific one-shot fix.

clusters.summary: No test exercises watchPodCIDRs=false with an actual PodCIDR change (the crash-suppression gate); if the `if n.watchPodCIDRs` guard were dropped/inverted, every non-LocalModeNodeCIDR proxy would crashloop and no test would catch it.

clusters.regrade_rationale: The watchPodCIDRs=false crash-suppression gate is load-bearing new behavior (every non-NodeCIDR proxy crashloops if it regresses) and the fix is one specific table row in the existing TestNodeManagerOnNodeChange.

clusters.regrade_rationale: Register-before-Start ordering is meta commentary on test fidelity; no demonstrated defect and no crisp one-shot fix, speculative race-masking.

clusters.summary: NodeManager.nodeLister is written at construction but never read afterward (only used inside the constructor's poll loop) — it could be a local variable, not struct state.

clusters.summary: NodeManager conflates five responsibilities (informer owner, startup poller, live snapshot provider, crash watchdog, config.NodeHandler) behind one type, and 'the current node' now lives in two places (NodeManager.node and NodeTopologyConfig.topologyLabels).
clusters.rationale: SRP/architecture opinion: NodeManager owns informer+poller+snapshot+watchdog+NodeHandler and 'current node' lives in two places — subjective design critique, no defect.
clusters.regrade_rationale: 'NodeManager conflates five responsibilities' is the calibration's explicit taste/architecture trivia example.

clusters.summary: The new startup poll loop retries silently with zero per-attempt diagnostic logging (the deleted getNodeIPs logged each failure), so a multi-minute startup stall gives operators no visibility.
clusters.rationale: The new PollUntilContextCancel loop logs nothing per attempt, whereas deleted getNodeIPs logged each failure; during a multi-minute startup stall operators get zero visibility — a real observability regression.
